questions.text: What is a shell company in the context of billing schemes?
options: A. A fictitious entity created to submit fraudulent invoices
B. A legitimate vendor that temporarily ceases operations
C. A subsidiary established for tax optimization purposes
D. A holding company that owns multiple operating entities
questions.justification_correct: A shell company in billing schemes is a fictitious entity created by a fraudster to submit fraudulent invoices to their employer. The company exists only on paper and provides no actual goods or services, allowing the perpetrator to divert company funds to themselves.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes a legitimate business experiencing operational changes, not a fraudulent entity. Option C describes a legitimate corporate structure used for lawful tax planning. Option D describes a legitimate parent company structure commonly used in business organizations.

questions.text: Which term describes an employee who appears on the payroll but does not actually work for the organization?
options: A. Ghost employee
B. Phantom contractor
C. Suspended worker
D. Inactive personnel
questions.justification_correct: A ghost employee is a fictitious person or a real person who does not actually work for the organization but is placed on the payroll. The fraudster collects the ghost employee's paychecks, making this a common form of payroll fraud.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is not standard terminology for payroll fraud schemes. Option C describes legitimate employees who may be temporarily suspended from work. Option D refers to employees who may be on leave or reassignment, not fraudulent payroll entries.

questions.text: In a pass-through billing scheme, what does the fraudster typically do?
options: A. Purchases goods through a shell company and resells them to the employer at a markup
B. Forwards legitimate invoices directly to accounts payable for payment
C. Transfers funds between multiple bank accounts to avoid detection
D. Returns purchased items for cash refunds after receiving reimbursement
questions.justification_correct: In a pass-through scheme, the fraudster sets up a shell company that purchases goods or services from legitimate vendors, then resells them to the employer at an inflated price. The shell company acts as a middleman, allowing the fraudster to collect the markup as profit.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes normal invoice processing, not a fraudulent scheme. Option C describes money laundering techniques unrelated to billing fraud. Option D describes a pay-and-return scheme, which is a different type of fraud involving expense reimbursements.

questions.text: What is check tampering?
options: A. The unauthorized interception and alteration of company checks
B. The delayed deposit of checks to maximize float time
C. The voiding of checks in the accounting system after issuance
D. The issuance of postdated checks to manage cash flow
questions.justification_correct: Check tampering involves the unauthorized interception, preparation, or alteration of checks drawn on the organization's bank accounts. This includes forging signatures, altering payees, or preparing fraudulent checks for the perpetrator's benefit.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes check kiting or float management, not tampering. Option C may be part of concealment but does not define the tampering itself. Option D describes a legitimate business practice for cash management.

questions.text: In payroll fraud, what is a falsified wages scheme?
options: A. An employee overstates hours worked or rate of pay to receive excess compensation
B. An employee receives wages while on unauthorized leave from work
C. An employer fails to pay employees the legally required minimum wage
D. An employee negotiates a higher salary without proper authorization
questions.justification_correct: Falsified wages schemes occur when employees overstate their hours worked, inflate their rates of pay, or otherwise manipulate payroll records to receive compensation they have not earned. This commonly involves altering timecards or falsifying overtime hours.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes absenteeism or time theft but not wage falsification. Option C describes an employer violation of wage laws, not employee fraud. Option D may represent an internal policy violation but not a fraudulent scheme involving falsified records.

questions.text: What characterizes a personal purchases billing scheme?
options: A. An employee uses company purchasing processes to buy items for personal use
B. An employee negotiates personal discounts with company vendors
C. An employee purchases office supplies at reduced bulk rates
D. An employee makes purchases on behalf of other employees
questions.justification_correct: In a personal purchases scheme, an employee uses the organization's purchasing system or accounts to acquire goods or services for their personal benefit, causing the company to pay for items that provide no business value. This is a form of asset misappropriation through fraudulent disbursements.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes a conflict of interest or policy violation but not necessarily fraud against the employer. Option C describes legitimate business purchasing practices. Option D may violate policy but does not constitute the employee personally benefiting from fraudulent disbursements.

questions.text: What is a pay-and-return scheme?
options: A. An employee makes a purchase, seeks reimbursement, then returns the item for a refund
B. An employee receives payment and then returns funds to avoid tax liability
C. An organization pays a vendor and later receives a volume discount rebate
D. An employee pays for business expenses and receives timely reimbursement
questions.justification_correct: In a pay-and-return scheme, an employee purchases an item with company funds or personal funds (then seeks reimbursement), and subsequently returns the merchandise for a refund. The employee keeps the refund while the company has already paid for the item, resulting in the employee receiving money without the company receiving value.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes a potential money laundering or tax evasion scenario, not an expense reimbursement fraud scheme. Option C describes a legitimate business rebate arrangement. Option D describes the normal, proper expense reimbursement process without fraudulent elements.

questions.text: Which control is most effective at preventing ghost employee schemes?
options: A. Independent verification of employee existence and distribution of paychecks
B. Requiring all employees to use direct deposit for payroll
C. Conducting annual performance reviews for all staff members
D. Implementing a formal employee orientation program
questions.justification_correct: Independent verification of employee existence (such as by HR or internal audit) and proper distribution of paychecks helps detect ghost employees. When someone independent from payroll preparation verifies that employees actually exist and work for the organization, fictitious employees are more likely to be discovered.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B may make it easier to perpetrate the fraud since direct deposit eliminates physical check distribution that could reveal ghosts. Option C focuses on performance management, not payroll fraud detection. Option D addresses onboarding processes but does not verify ongoing payroll accuracy or detect fictitious employees.

questions.text: In check tampering, what is a forged maker scheme?
options: A. The perpetrator forges an authorized signature on a company check
B. The perpetrator creates a fictitious vendor to receive payments
C. The perpetrator alters the amount on a properly signed check
D. The perpetrator steals signed blank checks from inventory
questions.justification_correct: A forged maker scheme occurs when a fraudster prepares a fraudulent check and forges the signature of an authorized maker (signer). The perpetrator creates the instrument without proper authorization and signs it themselves using a forged signature.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes a shell company billing scheme, not check tampering. Option C describes a check alteration scheme, which is different from forging the maker's signature. Option D may be part of other check tampering schemes but does not specifically define the forged maker scheme.

questions.text: What is a commission scheme in the context of payroll fraud?
options: A. An employee falsifies sales figures to increase commission payments
B. An employee shares commission revenue with other departments
C. An employer reduces commission rates without employee notification
D. An employee negotiates a higher commission rate with management
questions.justification_correct: A commission scheme is a type of payroll fraud where employees falsify sales or performance data to inflate their commission-based compensation. This may involve recording fictitious sales, overstating sales amounts, or manipulating other metrics tied to commission calculations.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes commission sharing arrangements which may violate policy but are not necessarily fraudulent. Option C describes a potential employer unfair labor practice, not employee fraud. Option D describes legitimate salary negotiation within an employment relationship.

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing vendor invoices and notices that a long-term supplier's invoices now show a different bank account for payments, though the vendor name and address remain the same. The account change was submitted via email, and no telephone verification occurred. What type of disbursement fraud scheme is most likely occurring?
options: A. An employee intercepted the vendor communication and substituted their own bank account details
B. The vendor legitimately changed banks but failed to follow proper notification procedures
C. A shell company has been created to duplicate the legitimate vendor's invoicing patterns
D. The company's accounts payable system has been compromised by external hackers
questions.justification_correct: This scenario describes an altered payee scheme, a form of check tampering where an employee intercepts legitimate vendor communications and substitutes fraudulent payment details to redirect funds to accounts they control. The lack of telephone verification of the banking change is a key control weakness that enables this fraud.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is possible but less likely given the lack of verification and the typical profile of such scenarios in fraud examinations. Option C describes a shell company scheme, but the vendor is legitimate and already established, not a fictitious entity created for fraud. Option D assumes external compromise without evidence, whereas most disbursement frauds are perpetrated by insiders with access to payment processing.

questions.text: During a routine audit, a CFE discovers that an employee in the purchasing department has been approving invoices from a vendor owned by the employee's spouse. The goods were actually delivered and properly priced at market rates. How should this situation be classified?
options: A. As a conflict of interest that violates proper business conduct even without financial harm
B. As a legitimate transaction since the pricing was fair and goods were received
C. As a shell company scheme because the vendor has a personal relationship with the employee
D. As expense reimbursement fraud due to the undisclosed relationship with the vendor
questions.justification_correct: This represents a conflict of interest, which is a type of corruption scheme under asset misappropriation. Even when transactions are conducted at fair market value and goods are delivered, the failure to disclose material relationships violates business ethics, procurement policies, and fiduciary duties. The employee's ability to influence vendor selection for personal benefit constitutes fraud regardless of pricing.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly assumes that fair pricing negates the fraudulent nature of undisclosed conflicts. Option C mischaracterizes the scheme; shell companies are fictitious entities, not legitimate businesses with undisclosed relationships. Option D incorrectly categorizes the fraud type, as expense reimbursement involves employee claims for business expenses, not vendor payments.

questions.text: A CFE analyzing payroll records notices that one employee consistently reports the maximum allowable overtime hours each pay period, but their supervisor's calendar shows they were attending out-of-office training during many of those overtime hours. What payroll fraud scheme is most likely present?
options: A. The employee is claiming overtime hours not actually worked, with or without supervisory collusion
B. The supervisor is operating a ghost employee scheme using the training time as cover
C. The employee is participating in a commission scheme based on training completion
D. The timesheet system has a technical error causing automated overtime calculations
questions.justification_correct: This scenario describes falsified hours or wages fraud, where an employee claims compensation for time not actually worked. The discrepancy between claimed overtime and documented off-site training indicates the employee is fraudulently inflating their hours. This may involve supervisory collusion if the supervisor is approving these timesheets, or inadequate supervisory review.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly identifies this as a ghost employee scheme, which involves payments to fictitious or terminated employees, not inflated hours for real employees. Option C misapplies commission schemes, which involve sales-based compensation fraud, not hourly wage manipulation. Option D assumes a system error without evidence, when the pattern suggests intentional fraud given the consistent maximum claims.

questions.text: A CFE reviewing expense reports finds that a sales manager submitted identical restaurant receipts for client entertainment in three different months, each time claiming meals with different clients. What is the most appropriate initial investigative step?
options: A. Interview the claimed clients to verify whether the business meals actually occurred
B. Expand the review to examine all of the manager's expense reports for additional duplicates
C. Immediately confront the sales manager to obtain an admission of wrongdoing
D. Report the findings to law enforcement as evidence of criminal fraud
questions.justification_correct: The most appropriate initial step is to expand the examination to determine the full scope of the fraudulent activity before taking action that might alert the subject. This follows proper fraud examination methodology: gather sufficient evidence to understand the scheme's extent, pattern, and duration before conducting interviews or making accusations. A broader review may reveal additional instances and strengthen the case.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A might provide useful information but could alert the subject or clients prematurely before the full scope is known. Option C violates proper investigative sequencing; confrontational interviews should occur only after evidence gathering is substantially complete. Option D is premature, as internal investigations should be completed and legal counsel consulted before involving law enforcement, and additional evidence is needed to support criminal referral.

questions.text: An accounts payable clerk has authorization to enter vendor invoices up to $5,000 but requires manager approval for higher amounts. A CFE discovers the clerk has been splitting larger purchases into multiple invoices just below the approval threshold. The goods were legitimately received. What scheme is this?
options: A. An internal control circumvention that enables potential fraud even if current transactions are legitimate
B. A permissible practice since each individual invoice is within the clerk's authorization limit
C. A billing scheme involving fictitious invoices designed to avoid detection thresholds
D. An expense reimbursement scheme that manipulates approval requirements
questions.justification_correct: Invoice splitting to circumvent approval thresholds is a fraudulent practice that defeats internal controls, even when the underlying purchases are legitimate. This behavior demonstrates intent to evade oversight and creates opportunity for future fraud. The deliberate structuring of transactions to avoid required approvals violates the organization's control framework and represents a red flag for potential or planned fraudulent activity.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly legitimizes control circumvention; deliberate splitting to avoid required approvals violates policy regardless of individual transaction authority. Option C misidentifies the scheme as the invoices represent real goods, not fictitious billing. Option D incorrectly categorizes this as expense reimbursement, which involves employee expense claims, not vendor invoice processing.

questions.text: A CFE examining payroll data at a manufacturing company finds that several employees in the same department show identical net pay amounts despite having different wage rates and hours worked. What is the most likely explanation for this anomaly?
options: A. Manual adjustments are being made to equalize pay, potentially indicating supervisory fraud
B. The employees have agreed to a job-sharing arrangement that equalizes their compensation
C. The payroll system has a rounding function that coincidentally produces identical amounts
D. The employees are all part of a collective bargaining unit with standardized pay
questions.justification_correct: Identical net pay for employees with different wage rates and hours worked is a significant red flag suggesting manual manipulation of payroll data. This could indicate various fraud schemes including a supervisor making unauthorized adjustments, potential ghost employee schemes where one person receives multiple paychecks, or falsified wage fraud. This anomaly warrants immediate detailed investigation of the specific payroll transactions and approval history.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is implausible because job-sharing typically doesn't result in identical net pay given different hours and rates, and such arrangements would be documented. Option C is highly unlikely as rounding functions operate on small amounts and wouldn't create identical net pay across different gross calculations. Option D doesn't explain the anomaly since unionized workers with different rates and hours would still have different net pay amounts.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating travel expense claims and finds that an employee consistently submits hotel receipts showing the full pre-tax amount but the credit card statements show a lower amount was actually charged due to corporate discounts applied at checkout. The employee receives reimbursement for the higher receipt amount. What scheme is occurring?
options: A. Overstated expense reimbursement fraud where the employee claims more than actually spent
B. A misunderstood reimbursement policy where the employee believed receipts controlled payment
C. A legitimate claim since the hotel receipt represents the actual posted room rate
D. Check tampering involving alteration of the reimbursement amounts
questions.justification_correct: This is overstated expense reimbursement fraud. The employee is claiming reimbursement for amounts greater than actually paid by submitting initial receipts that don't reflect discounts applied at checkout. The employee is obligated to seek reimbursement only for actual expenses incurred. The credit card statement showing lower charges proves the employee received discounts and is fraudulently claiming the difference.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B gives unwarranted benefit of the doubt; expense policies require reimbursement of actual costs, and the consistent pattern suggests intentional fraud rather than misunderstanding. Option C incorrectly interprets the standard; reimbursement should reflect actual out-of-pocket costs, not posted rates when discounts were received. Option D misidentifies the scheme; check tampering involves altering payment instruments, not inflating expense claims.

questions.text: During a fraud examination, a CFE discovers that a purchasing agent has been accepting kickbacks from a vendor in exchange for steering contracts to that vendor. The vendor's prices are approximately 15% higher than competitors. How should this scheme be classified?
options: A. As a corruption scheme involving kickbacks that caused financial harm through inflated pricing
B. As a billing scheme since the vendor submitted invoices for goods and services
C. As a conflict of interest only, since the goods were actually delivered to the company
D. As check tampering because the purchasing agent influenced payment processing
questions.justification_correct: This is a kickback scheme, a form of corruption under asset misappropriation. Kickbacks occur when an employee receives undisclosed payments from a vendor in exchange for business favors. The fact that the vendor charges above-market rates demonstrates economic harm to the organization. Unlike conflicts of interest that may not involve direct payments, kickbacks involve actual corrupt payments to influence business decisions, making this a more serious fraud category.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B misclassifies the scheme; billing schemes involve fraudulent invoicing, while this centers on corrupt payments influencing vendor selection. Option C understates the severity; kickbacks are more serious than simple conflicts of interest because they involve corrupt payments, not just undisclosed relationships. Option D incorrectly identifies the scheme; check tampering involves altering or intercepting payments, not receiving side payments from vendors.

questions.text: A CFE analyzing vendor payments notices that one vendor has received payments totaling $180,000 over the past year through eighteen separate $10,000 invoices, all just below the $10,001 threshold requiring competitive bidding. Each invoice describes vague services like 'consulting' or 'professional services.' What is the most significant fraud risk indicator present?
options: A. Structured transactions to avoid competitive bidding combined with vague descriptions suggest possible billing fraud
B. The vendor is providing ongoing services that naturally fall below the bidding threshold
C. The accounts payable department is efficiently processing invoices within approval limits
D. The consistent invoice amounts indicate a retainer agreement with predictable billing
questions.justification_correct: This scenario presents multiple red flags for billing fraud: deliberate structuring to avoid controls (competitive bidding requirements), vague service descriptions that are difficult to verify, and a pattern suggesting intentional threshold avoidance. These indicators together suggest a possible shell company scheme, personal purchases, or kickback arrangement. The combination of control circumvention and lack of specificity warrants immediate investigation of vendor legitimacy, service verification, and employee relationships.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B provides an innocent explanation that ignores the suspicious pattern of amounts precisely avoiding the control threshold and vague descriptions. Option C incorrectly characterizes the situation as proper processing when the pattern suggests intentional control evasion. Option D assumes a legitimate retainer without evidence, ignoring that retainers would typically be documented with specific service agreements rather than vague invoice descriptions.

questions.text: A CFE reviewing expense reimbursements finds that an employee submitted a claim for a business dinner with receipts showing alcohol purchases, which violates company policy prohibiting alcohol reimbursement. The employee manually redacted the itemized receipt to obscure the alcohol charges before submission. How should this be addressed?
options: A. As fraudulent expense reimbursement involving document alteration to claim prohibited expenses
B. As a minor policy violation that should be handled through employee counseling
C. As an honest mistake since the employee may not have understood the policy
D. As a documentation error that requires the employee to submit a corrected receipt
questions.justification_correct: Deliberately altering documentation to conceal prohibited expenses constitutes fraudulent expense reimbursement. The manual redaction demonstrates intentional deception to obtain unauthorized reimbursement. Document alteration elevates this beyond a simple policy violation to fraud, as it involves deliberate falsification of records to deceive the organization. This behavior indicates intent to circumvent controls and obtain money through deceptive means.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B significantly understates the severity; document alteration to obtain unauthorized payments is fraud, not a minor policy matter. Option C is implausible given the deliberate redaction; the intentional concealment demonstrates awareness of wrongdoing, not innocent misunderstanding. Option D mischaracterizes intentional fraud as a documentation error and would be inappropriate given the evidence of deliberate deception.

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing vendor records at a manufacturing company and discovers that ABC Supplies has submitted invoices for services that closely match the company's actual needs, but the invoices lack detailed descriptions of work performed. The CFE verifies that ABC Supplies has a valid business license and tax ID. However, when attempting to visit the vendor's listed address, the CFE finds it is a commercial mail receiving agency. What type of billing scheme should the CFE investigate first?
options: A. Shell company scheme
B. Nonconforming goods scheme
C. Short shipment scheme
D. Duplicate payment scheme
questions.justification_correct: A shell company scheme involves a fictitious entity created to submit fraudulent invoices for goods or services not actually provided. Key indicators include lack of detailed invoice descriptions, use of mail drops or commercial mail receiving agencies instead of physical business locations, and invoices that conveniently match company needs. The presence of a valid business license and tax ID does not preclude a shell company, as perpetrators often establish legal entities to appear legitimate while providing no actual goods or services.
questions.justification_wrong: A nonconforming goods scheme involves delivering goods that do not meet contract specifications, which does not match the lack of physical location evidence. A short shipment scheme involves delivering fewer goods than invoiced, but there is no evidence that goods were actually delivered at all in this scenario. A duplicate payment scheme involves submitting the same legitimate invoice multiple times for payment, whereas this scenario suggests the vendor itself may be fictitious rather than involving duplicate billing.

questions.text: During a fraud examination, a CFE notices that Employee A in the accounting department has been processing expense reimbursements that include restaurant receipts with handwritten totals that appear altered. The receipts show numbers that look traced over or darkened. The employee submitted these receipts for business meals during client meetings. What is the most appropriate initial step for the CFE to take?
options: A. Contact the restaurants to verify the actual transaction amounts
B. Immediately interview Employee A about the altered receipts
C. Review Employee A's personnel file for prior disciplinary issues
D. Calculate the total amount reimbursed over the past month
questions.justification_correct: When expense reimbursement fraud involving altered receipts is suspected, the CFE should first independently verify the actual transaction amounts by contacting the merchants directly. This establishes documentary evidence of the fraud before alerting the suspect. Independent third-party verification provides objective evidence that can substantiate or refute the suspicion of alteration, creating a factual foundation for the investigation before taking actions that might alert the subject or be influenced by subjective factors.
questions.justification_wrong: Immediately interviewing the employee would alert the suspect before evidence is secured and could compromise the investigation by allowing destruction of evidence or collusion. Reviewing personnel files for prior issues does not address the immediate need to verify whether fraud actually occurred in this instance. Calculating total amounts reimbursed provides scope information but does not verify whether the receipts were actually altered, which is the foundational question that must be answered first.

questions.text: A CFE reviewing payroll records discovers that an employee in the sales department has been paid commissions on the same customer orders in multiple pay periods. The employee is authorized to input sales data into the commission tracking system. When questioned informally, the employee claims the duplicate entries were honest mistakes due to system errors. What control would most effectively prevent this type of payroll fraud?
options: A. Implementing system controls that flag previously processed orders
B. Requiring all salespeople to submit written commission reports
C. Conducting annual background checks on sales employees
D. Establishing a commission cap for individual employees
questions.justification_correct: System controls that automatically flag or prevent duplicate processing of the same orders directly address the vulnerability exploited in this scheme. Automated controls that identify previously processed transactions prevent both intentional fraud and genuine errors by checking order numbers or transaction identifiers against historical records before calculating commissions. This is a preventive control that stops the fraud at the point of occurrence, regardless of whether the duplication is intentional or accidental.
questions.justification_wrong: Written commission reports add documentation but do not prevent duplicate entries from being processed if the employee controls data input and there is no systematic check for duplicates. Annual background checks are useful for hiring decisions but do not prevent ongoing fraud by current employees with system access. Commission caps might limit the total fraud amount but do not prevent the duplicate processing itself, and legitimate high performers might reach caps through proper sales, making this an ineffective control for this specific vulnerability.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a suspected billing scheme at a construction company. The CFE finds that invoices from XYZ Contractors include charges for premium materials, but project managers report that standard materials were delivered. The company controller, who approves all vendor payments, has a spouse who is listed as the registered agent for XYZ Contractors. What type of fraud scheme is most likely occurring?
options: A. A related-party billing scheme involving inflated invoices
B. A bid-rigging scheme involving contract manipulation
C. A progress billing scheme involving premature invoicing
D. A substitute material scheme involving inventory diversion
questions.justification_correct: This scenario describes a related-party billing scheme where an employee with payment approval authority has an undisclosed relationship with a vendor who submits inflated invoices. The controller's conflict of interest through the spousal relationship, combined with invoicing for premium materials while delivering standard materials, indicates intentional overbilling. The controller's position allows approval of fraudulent invoices from a vendor controlled by a family member, representing a classic conflict of interest combined with overbilling.
questions.justification_wrong: Bid-rigging schemes involve manipulating the competitive bidding process to favor a particular vendor, but the scenario describes overbilling rather than bid manipulation. Progress billing schemes involve invoicing for work not yet completed on long-term projects, whereas this involves charging for higher-quality materials than actually delivered. Substitute material schemes typically involve diverting delivered materials for personal use or resale, but this scenario indicates inferior materials were delivered as part of the billing fraud rather than proper materials being diverted afterward.

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a company employee has been submitting expense reimbursement requests for hotel stays during purported business travel. The CFE obtains the employee's corporate credit card statements and finds that the same hotels were paid directly by the company credit card on the same dates for which the employee submitted expense reimbursements. What type of expense reimbursement scheme is this?
options: A. A duplicate reimbursement scheme for already-paid expenses
B. A mischaracterized expense scheme involving personal travel
C. An overstated expense scheme using inflated receipts
D. A fictitious expense scheme using fabricated documentation
questions.justification_correct: This is a duplicate reimbursement scheme where an employee seeks reimbursement for expenses that were already paid by the company through another method, such as a corporate credit card. The employee is essentially being paid twice for the same expense. This scheme exploits poor coordination between different payment systems and lack of controls to verify that expenses were actually paid out-of-pocket by the employee before processing reimbursement.
questions.justification_wrong: A mischaracterized expense scheme involves claiming personal expenses as business-related, but the travel appears legitimate and was actually paid by the company. An overstated expense scheme involves inflating the actual cost of legitimate expenses, whereas here the expenses were legitimate but already paid. A fictitious expense scheme involves completely fabricated expenses that never occurred, but these hotel stays actually occurred and were business-related; the fraud is in seeking duplicate payment.

questions.text: A CFE reviewing check disbursements notices that several checks payable to established vendors were deposited into an account at a different bank than the vendors typically use. When the CFE contacts the vendors, they confirm they never received these payments. The checks bear what appear to be authorized signatures. What check tampering scheme should the CFE investigate?
options: A. Forged endorsement scheme where checks were redirected
B. Concealed check scheme where checks were hidden from records
C. Authorized maker scheme where checks were issued improperly
D. Intercepted check scheme where checks were stolen in mail
questions.justification_correct: A forged endorsement scheme involves intercepting checks legitimately made payable to vendors or other third parties, then forging the endorsement to deposit the checks into an account controlled by the perpetrator. The checks appear legitimate with proper signatures and payees, but the endorsement is fraudulent. This scheme differs from forged maker schemes because the checks themselves are properly authorized; the fraud occurs in the endorsement and deposit process.
questions.justification_wrong: A concealed check scheme involves hiding fraudulent checks from accounting records, but these checks appear in normal disbursement records. An authorized maker scheme involves someone with check-signing authority writing checks for fraudulent purposes, but the issue here is not with the issuance but with the interception and fraudulent endorsement. An intercepted check scheme is a general term that could apply, but forged endorsement more specifically identifies the mechanism of fraud—the forgery of the vendor's endorsement to redirect properly issued checks.

questions.text: A CFE analyzing payroll data at a retail company notices that one department has consistently higher average hours worked per employee compared to other similar departments. Further review reveals that the department supervisor has been clocking in for employees who arrived late or left early, then adjusting timecards to show full shifts. What control would best address this timecard fraud?
options: A. Implementing biometric time clocks that prevent buddy punching
B. Requiring timecards to be approved by human resources
C. Conducting surprise headcount verifications during shifts
D. Increasing the pay rate to reduce employee turnover
questions.justification_correct: Biometric time clocks that use fingerprints, facial recognition, or other unique biological identifiers prevent buddy punching and timecard fraud by ensuring that only the actual employee can clock in or out. This directly addresses the fraud mechanism where supervisors or coworkers clock in for absent or late employees. Biometric controls are preventive and automated, eliminating the ability to manipulate the time recording process at the point of entry.
questions.justification_wrong: Requiring HR approval of timecards adds a review layer but may not detect fraud if reviewers cannot verify actual attendance, and HR typically lacks direct knowledge of daily employee presence. Surprise headcount verifications are detective controls that might eventually catch discrepancies but do not prevent the fraud from occurring and are resource-intensive. Increasing pay rates addresses employee retention but does not prevent supervisors from manipulating timecards and may actually increase the financial impact of timecard fraud.

questions.text: During a fraud examination, a CFE finds that a purchasing agent has been approving invoices from a vendor that provides consulting services. The invoices contain vague descriptions such as "professional services" and "strategic consulting" without detailed breakdowns. The CFE confirms the vendor is a legitimate registered business owned by the purchasing agent's former college roommate. What should the CFE's primary concern be?
options: A. Whether the purchasing agent disclosed the relationship and recused from approval
B. Whether the vendor has adequate professional liability insurance
C. Whether the invoices were processed within standard payment terms
D. Whether the vendor is registered in the same state as the company
questions.justification_correct: The primary fraud concern in this scenario is the potential conflict of interest arising from the undisclosed personal relationship between the purchasing agent and the vendor. Even if the vendor is legitimate, the purchasing agent should have disclosed the relationship and recused themselves from the approval process to avoid impropriety. The vague invoice descriptions combined with the personal relationship create significant fraud risk, as the purchasing agent may be approving payments for services not rendered or overpriced services to benefit a friend.
questions.justification_wrong: Professional liability insurance is a legitimate business consideration but does not address the fraud risk posed by the undisclosed relationship and vague billing. Processing invoices within standard payment terms is an operational matter unrelated to whether fraud is occurring. The vendor's state of registration is a compliance issue but does not address whether the billing is fraudulent or whether the conflict of interest was properly managed.

questions.text: A CFE investigating expense reimbursement fraud finds that an employee has submitted multiple claims for the same business trip by using receipts from different portions of the trip separately. For example, the employee submitted a hotel folio showing total charges on one expense report, then submitted individual receipts for the room rate, parking, and meals from the same stay on subsequent reports. What detection method would most effectively identify this scheme?
options: A. Analyzing expense reports for overlapping dates and duplicate merchants
B. Requiring original receipts rather than photocopies
C. Establishing per-diem rates instead of actual expense reimbursement
D. Limiting the number of expense reports per employee per month
questions.justification_correct: Analyzing expense reports for overlapping dates and duplicate merchants is an effective detective control for identifying reimbursement fraud where the same expenses are claimed multiple times in different formats. This analytical approach would reveal that the same hotel on the same dates appears across multiple expense reports, or that individual line items sum to amounts already reimbursed via a master folio. This type of data analysis can be automated and routinely performed to detect patterns indicative of duplicate claims.
questions.justification_wrong: Requiring original receipts prevents some forms of expense fraud but does not stop this particular scheme, as the employee could obtain multiple original itemized receipts from the same merchant. Per-diem rates eliminate the need for receipts but may not be appropriate for all expense categories and represent a policy change rather than a detection method. Limiting expense report frequency does not prevent the fraud, as the employee could still include duplicate items within the allowed number of reports.

questions.text: A CFE reviews accounts payable records and discovers that a long-term vendor recently changed its payment address to a P.O. box, whereas payments were previously sent to a street address. The CFE also notes that recent invoices from this vendor have a different format and font than historical invoices. The accounts payable clerk who processed the address change was recently hired. What should the CFE do first?
options: A. Contact the vendor using previously established contact information to verify the change
B. Review the new accounts payable clerk's employment application
C. Examine all invoices processed by the new clerk for irregularities
D. Place a hold on all future payments to this vendor
questions.justification_correct: When vendor information changes are suspicious, the CFE should independently verify the change using previously established, trusted contact information rather than information provided in recent communications. This prevents falling victim to a scheme where fraudsters impersonate legitimate vendors by submitting false address changes to redirect payments. Contacting the vendor through historical channels (phone numbers, email addresses, or contacts from before the address change) allows verification of whether the vendor actually requested the change or whether an employee is attempting to divert payments.
questions.justification_wrong: Reviewing the clerk's employment application might reveal background information but does not address the immediate question of whether the vendor change is legitimate or fraudulent. Examining other invoices processed by the clerk could be a subsequent step but does not verify the specific concern about this vendor. Placing a hold on payments might be appropriate if fraud is confirmed, but doing so prematurely without verification could harm legitimate business relationships and the investigation should first establish whether the change is fraudulent.

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing vendor files and discovers that ABC Consulting Services has no tangible business presence, lists a post office box as its address, and has received payments totaling $47,000 over six months. The accounts payable clerk who set up the vendor also approves invoices for payment. What type of scheme is MOST likely occurring?
options: A. A shell company billing scheme
B. A pass-through billing scheme
C. A personal purchase scheme
D. A non-accomplice vendor scheme
questions.justification_correct: A shell company scheme involves creating a fictitious vendor entity that exists only on paper to fraudulently bill the victim organization. Key red flags include lack of physical business presence, post office box addresses, and the same employee handling both vendor setup and payment approval, which represents a segregation of duties violation that enables the fraud.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because pass-through schemes involve a legitimate vendor relationship where the fraudster marks up goods or services before reselling to the employer. C is incorrect because personal purchase schemes involve using company funds to buy items for personal use through legitimate vendors, not creating false entities. D is incorrect because non-accomplice vendor schemes involve overbilling by legitimate external vendors without employee collusion, whereas this scenario shows employee control over a fictitious entity.

questions.text: During a payroll fraud investigation, a CFE discovers that employee Jennifer Cole's net pay is being direct deposited into two different bank accounts—one in her name and another in the name of "J. Coleman." Personnel records show only one employment file for Cole. What scheme is MOST likely being perpetrated?
options: A. A ghost employee scheme
B. A falsified hours scheme
C. A payroll diversion scheme
D. A duplicate paycheck scheme
questions.justification_correct: A ghost employee scheme involves placing fictitious employees on the payroll to collect fraudulent paychecks. The use of a similar but different name (J. Coleman vs. Jennifer Cole) with a separate bank account, combined with only one legitimate employment file, indicates a fraudster has created a phantom worker to receive additional compensation.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because falsified hours schemes involve inflating work hours for real employees, not creating additional payment recipients. C is incorrect because payroll diversion typically refers to redirecting legitimate earned wages to unauthorized accounts, not creating false employees. D is incorrect because duplicate paycheck schemes involve processing multiple checks for the same legitimate employee's actual work period, whereas this involves creating an entirely separate fictitious worker identity.

questions.text: A CFE analyzing expense reports notices that sales manager Tom Harris submitted hotel receipts from a business trip to Dallas. Upon verification with the hotel, the CFE learns that Harris paid a discounted corporate rate of $89 per night, but his submitted receipts show $139 per night. What is the BEST description of this scheme?
options: A. An overstated expense reimbursement scheme
B. A mischaracterized expense scheme
C. A fictitious expense scheme
D. A duplicate reimbursement scheme
questions.justification_correct: An overstated expense reimbursement scheme occurs when an employee inflates the actual cost of legitimate business expenses to receive reimbursement exceeding what was actually spent. Harris actually stayed at the hotel (legitimate expense) but manipulated the receipt to show a higher nightly rate than he paid, pocketing the difference.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because mischaracterized expenses involve claiming personal expenses as business-related, not inflating the cost of legitimate business expenses. C is incorrect because fictitious expense schemes involve claiming reimbursement for expenses that never occurred, whereas Harris actually incurred hotel costs. D is incorrect because duplicate reimbursement involves submitting the same expense multiple times for payment, not inflating a single expense's cost.

questions.text: A company controller discovers that several checks issued to vendors were deposited into accounts different from those on file in the vendor master file. The check amounts and vendor names match approved invoices, but endorsements show deposits to accounts at different financial institutions. What type of check tampering is MOST likely occurring?
options: A. An intercepted check scheme
B. An authorized maker scheme
C. A concealed check scheme
D. A duplicate payment scheme
questions.justification_correct: An intercepted check scheme occurs when an employee steals a check intended for a legitimate payee and converts it for personal benefit, often by forging the payee's endorsement and depositing it into an account controlled by the fraudster. The checks were properly issued to legitimate vendors but diverted before reaching the intended recipients.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because authorized maker schemes involve employees with signature authority writing fraudulent checks, whereas these checks were properly authorized for legitimate vendors. C is incorrect because concealed check schemes focus on hiding the fraud in accounting records, not the physical interception and diversion of legitimate checks. D is incorrect because duplicate payment schemes involve processing and paying the same invoice multiple times, whereas this involves diverting single legitimate payments to unauthorized accounts.

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing procurement card transactions and identifies that purchasing agent Lisa Wong repeatedly makes purchases of $249 from an electronics retailer, just below the company's $250 threshold requiring manager approval. Over eight months, these transactions total $18,000. What control weakness is being exploited?
options: A. Inadequate monitoring of transactions below approval thresholds
B. Lack of physical inventory verification procedures
C. Missing vendor background check requirements
D. Insufficient reconciliation of purchasing card statements
questions.justification_correct: The fraudster is deliberately structuring transactions to remain just below the approval threshold, exploiting the control weakness that transactions below certain dollar amounts receive less scrutiny. Effective controls should include monitoring for patterns of transactions consistently near but below approval limits, as this behavior indicates potential threshold avoidance.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because while inventory verification might detect missing goods, the primary vulnerability being exploited is the lack of scrutiny for below-threshold transactions, not inventory controls. C is incorrect because vendor background checks would not prevent an employee from making repeated small purchases from a legitimate retailer. D is incorrect because while reconciliation is important, the specific weakness being exploited is the absence of pattern analysis and monitoring for transactions structured to avoid approval requirements.

questions.text: During a fraud examination, a CFE discovers that warehouse supervisor Mark Chen approved overtime hours for three subordinate employees who all received premium pay for working on a holiday when the facility was actually closed. Timekeeping records were manually adjusted by Chen. What prevention control would be MOST effective?
options: A. Requiring independent verification of overtime against facility access logs
B. Implementing biometric time clock systems
C. Conducting periodic surprise payroll audits
D. Requiring dual signatures on overtime authorizations
questions.justification_correct: Independent verification against objective evidence such as facility access logs, security badge swipes, or production records provides corroborating documentation that employees were actually present during claimed overtime periods. This control directly addresses the fraud by requiring evidence beyond the supervisor's approval that cannot be easily falsified.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because biometric systems only record clock-in times but do not prevent supervisors from manually overriding or falsifying hours worked, especially for salaried supervisors with system access. C is incorrect because periodic audits are detective controls that discover fraud after occurrence, not preventive controls that stop it. D is incorrect because dual signatures only add another approval layer but do not provide independent verification that the work actually occurred, and both signers could potentially collude.

questions.text: A CFE examining accounts payable notices that vendor Midwest Supply Company has received payments for identical invoice numbers on multiple occasions. Each duplicate invoice was submitted 60-90 days after the original, and all were processed by different AP clerks. What detective control failure MOST likely enabled this scheme?
options: A. Lack of automated duplicate invoice detection systems
B. Absence of vendor performance evaluations
C. Missing three-way match procedures
D. Inadequate vendor contract review processes
questions.justification_correct: Duplicate payment schemes are most effectively prevented through automated systems that flag matching invoice numbers, vendor names, amounts, and dates before processing payment. The fact that different clerks processed the duplicates suggests the organization lacks centralized or automated detection mechanisms that would identify previously paid invoices regardless of which clerk handles them.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because vendor performance evaluations assess service quality and delivery, not payment transaction integrity or duplicate invoice detection. C is incorrect because three-way matching (invoice, purchase order, receiving report) verifies that goods were ordered and received but does not prevent paying the same invoice multiple times. D is incorrect because contract review processes address pricing terms and obligations but do not detect duplicate submissions of the same invoice number in the payment system.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a suspicion that accounting manager Rachel Green is submitting personal expenses for reimbursement. Green has submitted numerous restaurant receipts claiming business meals with clients. What is the MOST effective method to verify whether these expenses are legitimate?
options: A. Contacting the purported clients to confirm the business meetings occurred
B. Comparing receipt timestamps to Green's calendar appointments
C. Analyzing the geographic locations of the restaurants claimed
D. Reviewing Green's corporate credit card statements for patterns
questions.justification_correct: Directly confirming with the alleged clients whether business meetings actually occurred provides the most reliable evidence to verify the legitimacy of claimed business meal expenses. This independent third-party verification either corroborates the business purpose or reveals that the meals were personal expenses mischaracterized as business-related.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because calendar appointments can be easily fabricated or entered retroactively by the fraudster to create false supporting documentation. C is incorrect because while unusual geographic patterns might raise suspicion, location alone does not confirm whether a meal was business or personal in nature. D is incorrect because credit card statement patterns might reveal spending habits but do not verify the business purpose of specific meals, and the fraud involves expense reimbursements which may be cash purchases, not credit card transactions.

questions.text: A CFE discovers that IT contractor invoices from TechSolutions LLC are consistently approved by department head Kevin Moore without supporting timesheets or work verification. TechSolutions' business registration shows Moore's home address. The invoices describe only vague services like "consulting" and "system maintenance." What is the PRIMARY red flag indicating fraud?
options: A. Undisclosed related party relationship between approver and vendor
B. Absence of competitive bidding documentation
C. Vague invoice descriptions lacking detail
D. Missing timesheets for contractor services
questions.justification_correct: The most significant red flag is the undisclosed related party relationship evidenced by the vendor's registration at the approving employee's home address. This represents a conflict of interest where Moore is both controlling the vendor entity and approving its payments, creating opportunity for fraudulent billing. Related party transactions require disclosure and independent oversight.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because while lack of competitive bidding may indicate procurement policy violations, the primary fraud indicator is the concealed ownership relationship between vendor and approver. C is incorrect because although vague descriptions are concerning and may facilitate fraud, they are secondary to the fundamental conflict created by the undisclosed related party relationship. D is incorrect because missing timesheets are a control weakness, but the core fraud risk stems from Moore's ability to approve payments to his own company without disclosure.

questions.text: A payroll manager is investigating why the department's total wage expense increased 12% despite no new hires or approved raises. A data analysis reveals that eight employees' hourly rates were increased in the system 30-60 days ago. All changes were made by payroll clerk Susan Torres, and most increases were under 5%. What should the manager do FIRST?
options: A. Verify whether proper authorization exists for each rate change
B. Interview Susan Torres about her payroll processing procedures
C. Calculate the total financial impact of the unauthorized changes
D. Review access logs to determine if Torres' credentials were compromised
questions.justification_correct: The first step should be verifying whether proper authorization documentation exists for the rate changes, such as approved personnel action forms or HR authorizations. This establishes whether the changes were legitimate or unauthorized, providing the factual foundation needed before taking further investigative or corrective actions. Documented authorization is required for all compensation changes.
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because interviewing the suspect before gathering evidence could alert Torres to the investigation, allowing destruction of evidence or collusion with others involved. C is incorrect because while calculating financial impact is important, determining the legitimacy of the changes through authorization verification must come first to establish whether fraud occurred. D is incorrect because reviewing access logs for credential compromise should occur after establishing that unauthorized changes were made, and the pattern suggests deliberate fraud rather than compromised credentials given the small, calculated increases.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating a billing scheme at a manufacturing company where the purchasing manager established a shell company that submits invoices for industrial supplies. The shell company's invoices contain legitimate product descriptions and market-rate pricing, and the goods are actually delivered to the company's warehouse. However, the products are then removed by the purchasing manager and sold to third parties. Which characteristic would BEST distinguish this scheme from a traditional shell company billing scheme?
options: A. The invoices reflect fair market value rather than inflated prices
B. The scheme involves actual delivery of goods to the company premises
C. The perpetrator diverts the delivered goods for personal financial gain
D. The shell company uses realistic product descriptions on invoices
questions.justification_correct: The critical distinguishing feature is that the perpetrator removes and converts the delivered goods for personal profit after receipt. While most shell company schemes involve payment for nonexistent goods or services, this hybrid scheme combines fraudulent billing with subsequent theft of inventory. The fraud occurs in two stages: the fraudulent disbursement to a shell entity and the subsequent conversion of company assets. This creates both a disbursement fraud and an inventory theft, making detection more complex because receiving documents appear legitimate.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect because some shell company schemes do use market-rate pricing to avoid detection through pricing analysis, though inflated pricing is more common. Option B is incorrect because the delivery of goods alone does not distinguish the scheme; what matters is what happens after delivery and who ultimately benefits. Option D is incorrect because using realistic product descriptions is a common characteristic of shell company schemes designed to avoid detection, not a distinguishing factor of this particular variation.

questions.text: An accounts payable clerk at a retail chain processes payments to vendors using a three-way match system requiring purchase orders, receiving reports, and invoices. The clerk has been making personal purchases from legitimate vendors using the company's vendor accounts, then intercepting the invoices and creating fraudulent purchase orders and receiving reports retrospectively to complete the match. Which control would be MOST effective in detecting this scheme?
options: A. Implementing vendor statement reconciliation by personnel independent of purchasing
B. Requiring sequential pre-numbering of all purchase orders in the system
C. Establishing dollar thresholds requiring supervisory approval for payments
D. Conducting periodic physical inventory counts compared to perpetual records
questions.justification_correct: Independent vendor statement reconciliation is most effective because it would reveal discrepancies between the vendor's record of transactions and the company's approved purchase orders. Vendors would show orders the company never authorized, and the reconciliation would identify purchases that lack proper front-end authorization. This control addresses the scheme at its source by verifying that all transactions with vendors were legitimately initiated by the company, regardless of whether supporting documentation was later fabricated.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because while pre-numbered purchase orders help ensure completeness and prevent skipped documents, they do not prevent someone with system access from creating fraudulent purchase orders with valid sequential numbers after the fact. Option C is incorrect because approval thresholds would not detect this scheme if the clerk keeps individual purchases below the threshold or forges approvals on the retrospectively created documents. Option D is incorrect because physical inventory counts would only be effective if the personally purchased items were supposed to be in inventory; if items were immediately removed or were non-inventory purchases (services, supplies consumed), this control would not detect the fraud.

questions.text: A nonprofit organization's development director operates a pass-through billing scheme where a vendor marks up services by 30% and pays the excess to a shell company controlled by the director. The vendor provides legitimate services at the inflated price, and the nonprofit receives the contracted work. During the investigation, the vendor claims they believed the arrangement was a legitimate referral fee. Which factor would MOST support a conspiracy charge against the vendor?
options: A. The vendor's pricing was significantly above documented market rates
B. The vendor made payments to an entity without verifiable business operations
C. The vendor failed to disclose the payment arrangement to the nonprofit
D. The vendor continued payments after learning of an internal investigation
questions.justification_correct: Making payments to an entity without verifiable business operations demonstrates the vendor's knowledge that the arrangement was illegitimate rather than a bona fide referral fee. A legitimate referral fee would be paid to an actual business providing identifiable services. The lack of verifiable operations indicates the vendor knew or should have known the shell company existed solely to funnel fraudulent proceeds. This knowledge element is critical to establishing the intent required for conspiracy, as it shows the vendor understood they were participating in a fraudulent scheme rather than a legitimate commercial arrangement.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect because pricing above market rates alone does not prove conspiracy; the vendor could argue they set their own prices without knowledge of the director's fraud. Option C is incorrect because vendors are generally not required to disclose their internal cost structures or profit-sharing arrangements to customers; non-disclosure does not necessarily establish criminal intent or knowledge of fraud. Option D is incorrect because while continuing payments after learning of an investigation might show consciousness of guilt, the question asks what would support the conspiracy charge, which requires agreement and intent at the time of the original acts, not subsequent behavior.

questions.text: A payroll supervisor at a healthcare facility with 200 employees creates ghost employee records for terminated workers by changing their direct deposit information to accounts she controls while maintaining their original personnel file termination dates. She then reinstates them in the payroll system with backdated rehire dates. Which analytical procedure would be MOST likely to detect this specific scheme?
options: A. Comparing the employee headcount per payroll to authorized positions in the budget
B. Analyzing gaps between termination dates in HR files and final paycheck dates
C. Testing for duplicate tax identification numbers across active employee records
D. Reconciling total payroll disbursements to the general ledger on a monthly basis
questions.justification_correct: Analyzing gaps between HR termination dates and final paycheck dates would reveal that employees continued receiving paychecks after their documented termination dates in the personnel files. Since the supervisor maintained the original termination dates in the HR files but backdated the rehires in the payroll system, this creates a detectable discrepancy between the two systems. Employees would show as terminated in HR records while simultaneously appearing as active and receiving payments in the payroll system, creating a clear red flag that these are ghost employees rather than legitimate workers.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect because if the ghost employees are being added within the total authorized headcount, or if the organization frequently operates below budgeted positions, this comparison would not detect the fraud. Option C is incorrect because the supervisor is reusing records of real terminated employees with their legitimate tax identification numbers rather than creating entirely fictitious identities with duplicate numbers. Option D is incorrect because general ledger reconciliation only verifies that recorded payroll amounts are properly posted to accounting records; it does not identify whether specific employees are legitimate or ghost employees.

questions.text: An operations manager at a distribution company submits expense reimbursement requests for meals during purported business trips. Investigation reveals the manager did take the trips and the meal receipts are genuine, but the meals were personal tourist activities rather than business purposes. The company's expense policy requires expenses to be "ordinary and necessary business expenses." Which element would be MOST critical to prove this constitutes reimbursement fraud?
options: A. The manager's knowledge that the expenses did not serve a business purpose
B. The manager's failure to obtain pre-approval for the specific meal expenses
C. The company's documentation showing the trips were approved for business purposes
D. The receipts showing the expense amounts exceeded per diem allowance limits
questions.justification_correct: The manager's knowledge that the expenses did not serve a business purpose is the critical element establishing fraudulent intent. Fraud requires not only a false representation (claiming personal meals were business expenses) but also scienter—knowledge of the falsity and intent to deceive. The manager's awareness that the meals were personal tourism rather than business-related demonstrates the intentional misrepresentation necessary for fraud. Without proving this knowledge element, the conduct might be characterized as a policy violation or negligent misclassification rather than intentional fraud.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the failure to obtain pre-approval is a procedural violation but does not establish fraudulent intent; many legitimate expenses are submitted without pre-approval depending on company policy. Option C is incorrect because while trip approval for business purposes provides context, it does not prove the manager knew the specific meal expenses were personal; the manager could argue they believed meals during business trips automatically qualified. Option D is incorrect because exceeding per diem limits is a policy violation regarding amount, but does not address whether the expense had a business purpose; an expense could exceed limits but still be legitimate, or fall within limits but be fraudulent.

questions.text: A accounts receivable clerk with access to incoming checks perpetrates a lapping scheme by applying Customer A's payment to Customer B's account, then applying Customer B's later payment to Customer A's account, continuing this rotation among multiple customers. The scheme has continued for 18 months. Which indicator would MOST likely appear first in the company's records as the scheme becomes unsustainable?
options: A. Increasing complaints from customers receiving statements showing incorrect balances
B. Growing number of accounts with payment amounts not matching invoiced amounts
C. Progressive lengthening of the average time between payment shifts among accounts
D. Systematic pattern of deposit timing that differs from customer payment patterns
questions.justification_correct: The progressive lengthening of time between payment shifts indicates the scheme is becoming unsustainable because the perpetrator is struggling to find payments of appropriate amounts to continue the rotation. As the scheme matures, it becomes increasingly difficult to match payment amounts and timing, forcing the perpetrator to hold misapplied payments longer before finding suitable payments to shift. This temporal extension is a mathematical inevitability in lapping schemes because the perpetrator must juggle an ever-growing number of misapplied payments, and the probability of receiving perfectly matched payments decreases over time.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect because sophisticated lapping perpetrators carefully match payment applications to avoid generating customer statements with incorrect balances; customers typically see their payments applied, just not to their own accounts. Option B is incorrect because perpetrators generally apply full payment amounts to accounts to avoid discrepancies; they shift which account receives which payment, but the amounts typically match. Option D is incorrect because deposits would still occur when customer payments arrive; the fraud involves which customer account is credited, not when deposits are made to the bank, so deposit timing would generally align with actual payment receipt patterns.

questions.text: A procurement officer at a university uses a billing scheme involving multiple shell companies with different names but sharing the same address and phone number. The officer structures invoices from each shell company to remain just under the $10,000 threshold requiring competitive bidding. Over two years, the total payments to these related entities exceed $400,000. Which legal theory would MOST likely apply to aggregate these payments for purposes of establishing the fraud magnitude?
options: A. The common scheme or plan doctrine allowing related transactions to be combined
B. The piercing the corporate veil theory treating separate entities as a single actor
C. The continuing offense doctrine extending the statute of limitations period
D. The conspiracy theory requiring proof of agreement among shell company principals
questions.justification_correct: The common scheme or plan doctrine allows prosecutors and civil plaintiffs to aggregate related fraudulent transactions as part of a single, ongoing scheme when they share common elements such as the same perpetrator, method, victims, and purpose. This doctrine is particularly applicable when a perpetrator deliberately structures transactions to evade detection thresholds. The multiple shell companies operating from the same location and controlled by the same person, all submitting invoices under the bidding threshold, demonstrate a unified fraudulent scheme rather than separate, unrelated transactions. This aggregation is crucial for establishing the true magnitude of the fraud and appropriate penalties.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because piercing the corporate veil is a remedy that disregards corporate separateness to hold individuals liable for corporate obligations, but it addresses liability rather than aggregating transaction amounts for measuring fraud magnitude. Option C is incorrect because the continuing offense doctrine relates to when the statute of limitations begins to run on ongoing criminal conduct, not to combining transaction amounts to measure fraud scale. Option D is incorrect because conspiracy requires proof of agreement between two or more persons; since the perpetrator controls all the shell companies alone, there is no separate conspiring party, and this theory does not address aggregating financial amounts.

questions.text: A sales director at a software company manipulates revenue recognition by creating shell companies that "purchase" licenses, then submits inflated expense reimbursement requests claiming travel to meet these fake customers. The shell companies never pay their invoices, but the initial sales are recorded as revenue, boosting the director's commission calculations. Which aspect of this scheme represents the MOST direct asset misappropriation?
options: A. The creation of fictitious revenue through shell company sales transactions
B. The expense reimbursement requests for travel to nonexistent customer meetings
C. The commission payments calculated on the basis of uncollectible receivables
D. The accounts receivable balance inflated by shell company unpaid invoices
questions.justification_correct: The expense reimbursement requests for fictitious travel represent the most direct asset misappropriation because they involve immediate, tangible theft of company cash through fraudulent disbursement claims. Asset misappropriation involves the theft or misuse of an organization's assets, and the false expense reimbursements directly result in cash leaving the company and going to the perpetrator. While the other elements involve financial statement fraud and improper commission calculations, the expense reimbursements are straightforward fraudulent disbursements that immediately and directly convert company funds to the perpetrator's personal benefit.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect because creating fictitious revenue is primarily financial statement fraud rather than asset misappropriation; it manipulates reported financial results but does not directly misappropriate assets. Option C is incorrect because while commission payments based on fake sales do result in asset misappropriation, they are derivative of the underlying revenue fraud and require the company's commission calculation system to process them, making them less direct than explicit false expense claims. Option D is incorrect because inflated accounts receivable is a financial statement misrepresentation rather than asset misappropriation; the receivables themselves are fictitious entries, not misappropriated assets.

questions.text: A controller at a manufacturing firm implements a check tampering scheme by intercepting vendor checks, using desktop publishing software to create replica checks with altered payee names, and depositing them into accounts he controls. He then voids the original checks in the accounting system and records them as check stock errors. Which forensic evidence would provide the STRONGEST proof of the alteration?
options: A. Bank records showing deposits to accounts controlled by the perpetrator
B. Sequential check numbers showing gaps in the cleared check sequence
C. Examination of check paper stock revealing different paper composition
D. Voided check records showing unusual patterns of check stock errors
questions.justification_correct: Examination revealing different paper composition provides the strongest physical proof of alteration because it demonstrates that the deposited checks are not authentic company checks but rather fraudulent replicas. While desktop publishing software can replicate visual elements like fonts, logos, and layouts, it cannot replicate the specific paper stock, security features, chemical composition, and physical characteristics of genuine company check stock. This forensic evidence is objective, scientific, and difficult to refute, directly proving that the checks are fabricated rather than legitimately issued company instruments.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect because while deposits to perpetrator-controlled accounts show the destination of funds and establish benefit, they do not prove the checks themselves were altered; the perpetrator could argue the payments were legitimate. Option B is incorrect because gaps in check sequences might indicate missing checks but do not prove alteration; gaps could result from legitimately voided checks, and the perpetrator did void the originals in the system. Option D is incorrect because while unusual patterns of check stock errors may raise suspicion and provide investigative leads, pattern analysis alone does not constitute physical proof that specific checks were altered or fabricated.

questions.text: An employee benefits manager administers the company's tuition reimbursement program, which pays employees' educational expenses up to $5,000 annually. The manager submits claims for online courses she never took, using fabricated completion certificates and receipts from a website she created that mimics a legitimate online university. The actual university has no record of her enrollment. Which control testing procedure would be MOST effective in detecting this scheme?
options: A. Requiring employees to submit official transcripts directly from educational institutions
B. Implementing a policy requiring pre-approval of courses before enrollment
C. Analyzing reimbursement patterns for statistical anomalies in claim frequency
D. Establishing annual limits on the total amount available for reimbursement
questions.justification_correct: Requiring official transcripts sent directly from educational institutions to the employer is most effective because it establishes independent, third-party verification that cannot be fabricated by the employee. When transcripts are sent directly from the institution's registrar, it bypasses the employee's ability to create fraudulent documentation. The actual university would have no record of the manager's enrollment, so they could not provide transcripts, immediately exposing the fraud. This control creates an unbreakable verification chain from an independent source beyond the perpetrator's control.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because while pre-approval ensures courses are authorized before reimbursement, it does not verify that the employee actually completed the approved courses; the manager could still submit fabricated completion documentation for pre-approved courses. Option C is incorrect because analyzing statistical patterns might identify anomalies like unusual claim frequency, but the manager could structure claims to appear normal, and patterns alone do not verify whether specific claimed courses were actually taken. Option D is incorrect because annual limits control the amount of reimbursement but do nothing to verify the legitimacy of claims; the manager could still submit fraudulent claims up to the established limit.
